subject: Science

unit.unitOverview: Look up at the sky tonight. If it is clear over Mogadishu, Hargeisa, or the beaches near Mombasa, you will see a huge dark blanket sprinkled with tiny lights, and often a bright, glowing Moon. Have you ever wondered why the Moon is sometimes a thin curved sliver, like the tip of a fingernail, and sometimes a full round circle as bright as a lamp? Have you wondered why the Sun comes up in the morning and goes down at night, so that we have day and then darkness? In this unit you are going to become a real sky scientist, called an astronomer, and you will learn the answers.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, experiments and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured directly from the Stage 3 science workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: By the end of this unit, if you study carefully and try the activities, you will be able to do all of these things. Read them now, and read them again at the end to see how much you have grown.
Describe Earth, the Sun, and the Moon as spherical (ball-shaped) objects, not flat.
Compare the sizes of Earth, the Sun, and the Moon, and say which is biggest and which is smallest.
Explain that Earth spins on its axis and that this spinning causes day and night.
Explain that Earth orbits the Sun once each year.
Describe how the Moon orbits Earth about once every month.
Name and put in order the main phases of the Moon, from new Moon to full Moon and back.
Explain why the Moon appears to change shape, using the idea of sunlight and shadow.
Record your own careful observations of the Moon over several nights, like a real scientist.
Use science words such as orbit, rotate, phase, sphere, crescent, and gravity correctly.

concepts.title: Everything in the Sky is Round Like a Ball
concepts.explanation: Let us begin with a big idea. Earth, the Sun, and the Moon are all shaped like balls. We call this shape spherical, which just means round like a football or an orange. Long ago, before people had cameras and rockets, some thought Earth was flat like a giant plate. But clever observers noticed clues that showed Earth is really a giant ball, and today we have photographs taken from space that show Earth as a beautiful blue and white sphere floating in the dark. How can we tell that Earth is round without flying…
concepts.example: There are more clues. When you climb up high, to the top of a tall hill in the Rift Valley, you can see much farther than when you stand on flat ground, because you are looking over the curve of the Earth. Travellers…

concepts.title: Big, Bigger, Biggest: Sizes in Space
concepts.explanation: Now let us think about size. Which is biggest: Earth, the Sun, or the Moon? Many children guess wrongly, because in the sky the Sun and the Moon look almost the same size, like two small coins held up high. But that is a trick of distance. The truth is that the Sun is by far the biggest, then Earth is in the middle, and the Moon is the smallest of the three. Here is a way to picture it using things from around your home. Imagine the Moon is a small marble, or a single date. Then Earth would be about the size of an…
concepts.example: So why do the Sun and the Moon look about the same size when we look up? It is a wonderful coincidence. The Sun is huge but extremely far away. The Moon is tiny but very close to us. The bigness of the Sun and its great…

concepts.title: Why We Have Day and Night
concepts.explanation: Every single day the Sun seems to rise in the east, climb across the sky, and set in the west. It looks as if the Sun is moving around us. But here is the surprising truth: the Sun is not travelling around Earth each day. Instead, Earth is spinning. This spinning is called rotation. Picture Earth as a giant ball turning slowly around an imaginary line that runs through its middle from top to bottom. That imaginary line is called the axis. Earth turns around this axis like a spinning top or like a globe you push…
concepts.example: Now think about the Sun shining on this spinning ball. The Sun can only light up the half of Earth that is facing toward it. On that half it is bright: that is day. The other half of Earth is turned away, into its own…

concepts.title: The Moon, Earth's Faithful Companion
concepts.explanation: The Moon is Earth's closest neighbour in space and its natural companion. While Earth orbits the Sun, the Moon orbits Earth. It takes the Moon about one month — roughly 29 or 30 days — to travel all the way around Earth one time. As it circles us, it keeps us company through every night. Here is something surprising and important: the Moon does not make its own light. The Moon has no fire and no glow of its own. It is a ball of grey rock. The reason the Moon shines so brightly at night is that the Sun's light…
concepts.example: Another gentle wonder: we always see the same face of the Moon. The Moon turns in such a perfect way as it orbits that the same side is always pointing toward Earth. So the patterns of light and dark you see on the Moon…

concepts.title: The Phases of the Moon
concepts.explanation: Now we come to one of the most beautiful patterns in the whole sky: the phases of the Moon. If you watch the Moon night after night for a month, you will see it slowly change shape. First there is no Moon at all, or only a whisker-thin curve. Then a thin crescent appears. Night by night the bright part grows fatter, until half the Moon is lit, then almost all of it, until at last a glorious round full Moon shines. Then it shrinks back again, night by night, until it disappears, and the whole story begins once…
concepts.example: Here are the main phases in order, so you can follow the story: new Moon (dark and hidden), then a thin waxing crescent (a growing sliver), then the first quarter (half lit), then a fat gibbous shape, then the bright…

concepts.title: The Moon, the Tides, and the Ocean
concepts.explanation: The Moon does not only give us light. Its gravity — that same invisible pull — reaches all the way to Earth and gently tugs on our oceans. This pull helps create the tides, which are the slow rising and falling of the sea along the coast. If you have visited the shore at Mombasa, Lamu, or Dar es Salaam, you may have noticed that sometimes the water comes far up the beach (high tide) and other times it pulls back and leaves wide wet sand and rock pools (low tide). This gentle in-and-out breathing of the sea happens…
concepts.example: The Moon does not only give us light. Its gravity — that same invisible pull — reaches all the way to Earth and gently tugs on our oceans. This pull helps create the tides, which are the slow rising and falling of the…

explorations.title: Making Day and Night With a Ball and a Torch
explorations.context: To model how Earth's spinning causes day and night, and to see why one side is light while the other is dark.
explorations.prompt: Before you start, write down what you think: When I shine the torch on one side of the ball, what will the other side look like? As I turn the ball, what will happen to a spot marked on it?
explorations.answer: Was the whole ball ever lit up at once? Why not? What made your town go from day into night — the torch moving, or the ball turning? How does this model explain why it is night on the far side of the world when it is day where you live?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To model how Earth's spinning causes day and night, and to see why one side is light while the other is dark.

explorations.title: Modelling the Phases of the Moon
explorations.context: To see for yourself why the Moon appears to change shape, and to make the phases of the Moon with a ball and a torch.
explorations.prompt: Write down your idea: As the ball (Moon) moves around my head (Earth), will the lit part I can see stay the same, or change? Why do I think so?
explorations.answer: Did the ball itself ever really change shape, or did only the lit part you could see change? When did you see the whole lit side (full Moon) — when the torch was in front of you or behind you? How does this model explain why the real Moon seems to change shape over a month?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To see for yourself why the Moon appears to change shape, and to make the phases of the Moon with a ball and a torch.

explorations.title: Your Own Moon-Watching Diary
explorations.context: To observe the real Moon over many nights, like a true astronomer, and to discover the pattern of its phases with your own eyes.
explorations.prompt: Write your prediction: Over the next two weeks, do I think the Moon will stay the same shape, or slowly change? If it changes, will it get bigger or smaller first?
explorations.answer: Did the Moon change shape from night to night? Describe how it changed. Did the lit part grow bigger (waxing) or shrink smaller (waning) during your two weeks? Can you name any of the phases you drew, such as crescent, quarter, or full Moon?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To observe the real Moon over many nights, like a true astronomer, and to discover the pattern of its phases with your own eyes.

explorations.title: How Far, How Big? A Size Model of Earth and Moon
explorations.context: To compare the sizes of Earth and the Moon using objects from your kitchen, and to understand that Earth is much bigger than the Moon.
explorations.prompt: Write your idea: If Earth is the size of an orange, do I think the Moon would be the size of a date, a marble, or another orange? Which one?
explorations.answer: Which object was the best size to be the Moon next to your Earth-orange? Why? Is the Earth bigger or smaller than the Moon? By about how much? Why do you think the Moon looks so big in the night sky, even though it is smaller than Earth?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To compare the sizes of Earth and the Moon using objects from your kitchen, and to understand that Earth is much bigger than the Moon.

methods.title: Making Day and Night With a Ball and a Torch
methods.example: To model how Earth's spinning causes day and night, and to see why one side is light while the other is dark.
steps: Stick the sticker on one spot of the ball. This spot is your home town, such as Mogadishu.
Make the room dark. Ask a helper to hold the torch still and shine it at the ball from one side. The torch is the Sun.
Notice that only the half of the ball facing the torch is lit. The other half is in shadow.
Turn the ball slowly around, keeping the torch still. Watch your sticker move from the lit side into the shadow and back again.
Say "day" out loud when your sticker is in the light, and "night" when it is in the shadow.

methods.title: Modelling the Phases of the Moon
methods.example: To see for yourself why the Moon appears to change shape, and to make the phases of the Moon with a ball and a torch.
steps: Darken the room. Place the torch on a table so it shines steadily across the room. This is the Sun.
Hold the ball out at arm's length. Your head is Earth, and the ball is the Moon.
Turn so the ball is directly between your face and the torch. Look at the side of the ball facing you — it is dark. This is the new Moon.
Turn slowly to your left, keeping the ball up. Watch a thin curved sliver of light appear on the ball. This is the crescent.
Keep turning. When the torch is to your side, half the ball you see is lit. This is the first quarter.

methods.title: Your Own Moon-Watching Diary
methods.example: To observe the real Moon over many nights, like a true astronomer, and to discover the pattern of its phases with your own eyes.
steps: Choose a time each evening when you can see the Moon, at roughly the same time each night. Always go with an adult.
Each clear night, look at the Moon and carefully draw its shape in your notebook.
Write down the date and the time next to your drawing.
Note where the Moon is in the sky — low near the horizon, or high up.
Do this for as many nights as you can over two to four weeks. On cloudy nights, simply write "cloudy — no Moon seen."

methods.title: How Far, How Big? A Size Model of Earth and Moon
methods.example: To compare the sizes of Earth and the Moon using objects from your kitchen, and to understand that Earth is much bigger than the Moon.
steps: Place the orange on the table. This is your Earth.
Beside it, place a date stone, a marble, and a chickpea in a row.
The real Moon is about one quarter as wide as Earth. Look at your Earth-orange and choose the object that looks about one quarter as wide.
Hold your chosen Moon next to the Earth-orange and compare them.
Now place your Moon-object about one metre (a big step) away from the orange to show that the Moon is far from Earth, not touching it.

workedExamples.title: Counting the Days of the Lunar Month
workedExamples.prompt: Astronomers and families use the Moon to count time, so let us practise a little counting together, step by step.
workedExamples.solution: Question: The new crescent Moon is sighted, marking the first day of the month. About how many days later will the Moon be full and round? And about how many days after that will it return to a new Moon again? Step 1 — Remember the whole cycle. One complete lunar month, from new Moon to the next new Moon, takes about 29 to 30 days. Let us use 30 days to keep our counting simple. Step 2 — Find the full Moon. The full Moon happens about halfway through the cycle, because that is when the Moon has travelled halfway…

workedExamples.title: Guided example 2
workedExamples.prompt: Which of these is the biggest? (a) the Earth (b) the Sun (c) the Moon (d) they are all the same
workedExamples.solution: 2 b — the Sun The Sun is by far the biggest; over a million Earths could fit inside it.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 3
workedExamples.prompt: Which of these is the smallest? (a) the Earth (b) the Sun (c) the Moon (d) they are all the same
workedExamples.solution: 3 c — the Moon The Moon is the smallest of the three, about one quarter as wide as Earth.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 4
workedExamples.prompt: What causes day and night on Earth? (a) the Sun moving around Earth (b) Earth spinning on its axis (c) the Moon blocking the Sun (d) clouds covering the Sun
workedExamples.solution: 4 b — Earth spinning Earth rotates on its axis, carrying us into and out of sunlight.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 5
workedExamples.prompt: How long does Earth take to spin around once? (a) 1 hour (b) 12 hours (c) 24 hours (d) one week
workedExamples.solution: 5 c — 24 hours One full spin takes 24 hours, which is one day and night.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 6
workedExamples.prompt: How long does Earth take to orbit the Sun once? (a) one day (b) one week (c) one month (d) one year
workedExamples.solution: 6 d — one year Earth's orbit around the Sun takes about 365 days, which is one year.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 7
workedExamples.prompt: How long does the Moon take to orbit the Earth once? (a) one day (b) one week (c) about one month (d) one year
workedExamples.solution: 7 c — about one month The Moon orbits Earth in roughly 29 to 30 days.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 8
workedExamples.prompt: Why does the Moon shine at night? (a) it is on fire (b) it makes its own light (c) it reflects the Sun's light (d) it is covered in lamps
workedExamples.solution: 8 c — reflects the Sun's light The Moon is grey rock with no light of its own; it acts like a mirror.

practice.prompt: What shape are the Earth, the Sun, and the Moon? (a) flat like a plate (b) square like a box (c) spherical like a ball (d) triangular
practice.answer: 1 c — spherical All three are round balls, not flat. Photos from space and everyday observation show this.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which of these is the biggest? (a) the Earth (b) the Sun (c) the Moon (d) they are all the same
practice.answer: 2 b — the Sun The Sun is by far the biggest; over a million Earths could fit inside it.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which of these is the smallest? (a) the Earth (b) the Sun (c) the Moon (d) they are all the same
practice.answer: 3 c — the Moon The Moon is the smallest of the three, about one quarter as wide as Earth.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: What causes day and night on Earth? (a) the Sun moving around Earth (b) Earth spinning on its axis (c) the Moon blocking the Sun (d) clouds covering the Sun
practice.answer: 4 b — Earth spinning Earth rotates on its axis, carrying us into and out of sunlight.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: How long does Earth take to spin around once? (a) 1 hour (b) 12 hours (c) 24 hours (d) one week
practice.answer: 5 c — 24 hours One full spin takes 24 hours, which is one day and night.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: How long does Earth take to orbit the Sun once? (a) one day (b) one week (c) one month (d) one year
practice.answer: 6 d — one year Earth's orbit around the Sun takes about 365 days, which is one year.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: How long does the Moon take to orbit the Earth once? (a) one day (b) one week (c) about one month (d) one year
practice.answer: 7 c — about one month The Moon orbits Earth in roughly 29 to 30 days.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Why does the Moon shine at night? (a) it is on fire (b) it makes its own light (c) it reflects the Sun's light (d) it is covered in lamps
practice.answer: 8 c — reflects the Sun's light The Moon is grey rock with no light of its own; it acts like a mirror.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: What do we call the different shapes the Moon seems to have? (a) Moon colours (b) Moon phases (c) Moon sizes (d) Moon spots
practice.answer: 9 b — Moon phases The changing shapes are called phases.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: What holds the Moon near the Earth and stops it drifting away? (a) magnetism (b) gravity (c) wind (d) string
practice.answer: 10 b — gravity Gravity is the invisible pull that keeps the Moon near Earth.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: When it is bright midday where you live, on the opposite side of Earth it is about: (a) also midday (b) midnight (c) morning (d) sunset
practice.answer: 11 b — midnight The opposite side of the spinning Earth faces away from the Sun, so it is night.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: The thin crescent Moon in the evening sky is important to Muslim families because it can mark: (a) the start of the school day (b) the beginning of Ramadan (c) the end of the year (d) high tide
practice.answer: 12 b — beginning of Ramadan The Islamic lunar calendar begins each month with the new crescent.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

activities.title: Making Day and Night With a Ball and a Torch
activities.materials: A round ball, an orange, or a grapefruit (this is your Earth); A torch, or a phone light, or a lamp (this is your Sun); A small sticker, or a spot drawn with a marker (this is your town); A darkened room
steps: Stick the sticker on one spot of the ball. This spot is your home town, such as Mogadishu.
Make the room dark. Ask a helper to hold the torch still and shine it at the ball from one side. The torch is the Sun.
Notice that only the half of the ball facing the torch is lit. The other half is in shadow.
Turn the ball slowly around, keeping the torch still. Watch your sticker move from the lit side into the shadow and back again.
Say "day" out loud when your sticker is in the light, and "night" when it is in the shadow.

activities.title: Modelling the Phases of the Moon
activities.materials: A small ball, an orange, or a ball of foil (this is the Moon); A bright torch or lamp (this is the Sun); A darkened room; Your own head in the middle (this is the Earth — you are standing on Earth!)
steps: Darken the room. Place the torch on a table so it shines steadily across the room. This is the Sun.
Hold the ball out at arm's length. Your head is Earth, and the ball is the Moon.
Turn so the ball is directly between your face and the torch. Look at the side of the ball facing you — it is dark. This is the new Moon.
Turn slowly to your left, keeping the ball up. Watch a thin curved sliver of light appear on the ball. This is the crescent.
Keep turning. When the torch is to your side, half the ball you see is lit. This is the first quarter.

activities.title: Your Own Moon-Watching Diary
activities.materials: A notebook or some sheets of paper; A pencil; A calendar to note the dates; A grown-up to watch the sky with you
steps: Choose a time each evening when you can see the Moon, at roughly the same time each night. Always go with an adult.
Each clear night, look at the Moon and carefully draw its shape in your notebook.
Write down the date and the time next to your drawing.
Note where the Moon is in the sky — low near the horizon, or high up.
Do this for as many nights as you can over two to four weeks. On cloudy nights, simply write "cloudy — no Moon seen."

activities.title: How Far, How Big? A Size Model of Earth and Moon
activities.materials: One orange or a medium ball (this is Earth); One small item such as a date stone, a marble, a chickpea, or a small pebble (choices for the Moon); A ruler or tape measure (optional); A table to lay them on
steps: Place the orange on the table. This is your Earth.
Beside it, place a date stone, a marble, and a chickpea in a row.
The real Moon is about one quarter as wide as Earth. Look at your Earth-orange and choose the object that looks about one quarter as wide.
Hold your chosen Moon next to the Earth-orange and compare them.
Now place your Moon-object about one metre (a big step) away from the orange to show that the Moon is far from Earth, not touching it.

activities.title: Observe: The Phases of the Moon
activities.materials: Notebook, pencil and safe things you can find at home
steps: Look for an example of the phases of the moon around your home or outside.
Watch it closely for a few minutes and note three things you notice.
Write or draw what you observed, using the unit's science words.
Explain your finding to a family member or your teacher.

activities.title: Sort: The Moon, the Tides, and the Ocean
activities.materials: Notebook, pencil and safe things you can find at home
steps: Look for an example of the moon, the tides, and the ocean around your home or outside.
Find examples at home and sort them into groups, then explain your rule.
Write or draw what you observed, using the unit's science words.
Explain your finding to a family member or your teacher.

terms: Earth orbits the Sun once (a year)
Moon orbits Earth once (a lunar month)
Number of planets in the solar system
A shape the Moon appears to have.
A thin curved slice of Moon.
When the Moon looks dark and hidden.
When the Moon looks like a bright full circle.
The invisible pull that holds objects together.

vocabulary.example: 🤖 Ask Your AI Tutor Whenever you forget a fact from this card, just ask me.
Explain that Earth orbits the Sun once each year.
Name and put in order the main phases of the Moon, from new Moon to full Moon and back.

vocabulary.term: Number of planets in the solar system

vocabulary.meaning: A shape the Moon appears to have.
vocabulary.example: Use science words such as orbit, rotate, phase, sphere, crescent, and gravity correctly.

vocabulary.meaning: A thin curved slice of Moon.
vocabulary.example: The thin crescent Moon is very special to Muslims all over the world.

vocabulary.meaning: When the Moon looks dark and hidden.
vocabulary.example: The Islamic calendar, called the Hijri calendar, follows the Moon, so every month begins with a new Moon.

vocabulary.meaning: When the Moon looks like a bright full circle.
vocabulary.example: Name and put in order the main phases of the Moon, from new Moon to full Moon and back.

vocabulary.meaning: The invisible pull that holds objects together.
vocabulary.example: Use science words such as orbit, rotate, phase, sphere, crescent, and gravity correctly.

commonMistakes: Saying the Sun moves around Earth to make day.
It looks that way, but the Sun stays still.
Saying the Moon makes its own light.
The Moon is grey rock with no light of its own.
Saying the Moon really changes shape.
The Moon is always a full ball.
Ships sink hull-first over the horizon and photos show a round Earth.
Saying the Moon is bigger than the Earth.
The Moon only looks big because it is close.

connections.text: Gravity, the pulling force you met before, is what holds the Moon near Earth and keeps us on the ground.
The Moon shines by reflecting light, and day and night are about light and shadow on a spinning Earth.
The lunar calendar, the Ramadan and Eid crescent, and reflecting on the order of creation all connect to this unit.
Understanding the Earth as a sphere, the oceans, and the tides links science to the world map.
Counting the days of the lunar month and comparing sizes uses your number skills.
Keep a Moon diary for a whole month and see the complete cycle of phases.

fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: 9 b — Moon phases The changing shapes are called phases.

fluency.prompt: What holds the Moon near the Earth and stops it drifting away? (a) magnetism (b) gravity (c) wind (d) string
fluency.answer: 10 b — gravity Gravity is the invisible pull that keeps the Moon near Earth.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: 10 b — gravity Gravity is the invisible pull that keeps the Moon near Earth.

fluency.prompt: When it is bright midday where you live, on the opposite side of Earth it is about: (a) also midday (b) midnight (c) morning (d) sunset
fluency.answer: 11 b — midnight The opposite side of the spinning Earth faces away from the Sun, so it is night.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: 11 b — midnight The opposite side of the spinning Earth faces away from the Sun, so it is night.

fluency.prompt: The thin crescent Moon in the evening sky is important to Muslim families because it can mark: (a) the start of the school day (b) the beginning of Ramadan (c) the end of the year (d) high tide
fluency.answer: 12 b — beginning of Ramadan The Islamic lunar calendar begins each month with the new crescent.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: 12 b — beginning of Ramadan The Islamic lunar calendar begins each month with the new crescent.

realProblems.prompt: Use what you have learned to answer these real-life questions.
realProblems.answer: 1 She should look toward the west, just after sunset. The new crescent appears low in the western sky in the early evening. The thin new Moon follows the Sun down and is only briefly visible after sunset.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 1 She should look toward the west, just after sunset. The new crescent appears low in the western sky in the early evening. The thin new Moon follows the Sun down and is only briefly visible after sunset.

realProblems.prompt: Halima wants to look for the new Ramadan crescent. Should she look toward the east or the west, and at what time of day? Explain.
realProblems.answer: 2 No. When it is midday in Mogadishu, the opposite side of Earth is in night, because only the half facing the Sun has day. Earth is a spinning ball; only one half is lit at a time.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 2 No. When it is midday in Mogadishu, the opposite side of Earth is in night, because only the half facing the Sun has day. Earth is a spinning ball; only one half is lit at a time.

realProblems.prompt: It is bright midday in Mogadishu. Hassan says it must be daytime everywhere on Earth. Is he right? Explain why or why not.
realProblems.answer: 3 It is called the tide (high tide and low tide). It is mostly caused by the Moon's gravity pulling on the ocean water. The Moon's pull makes the sea rise and fall along the coast.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 3 It is called the tide (high tide and low tide). It is mostly caused by the Moon's gravity pulling on the ocean water. The Moon's pull makes the sea rise and fall along the coast.

realProblems.prompt: A fisherman at Lamu notices the sea rising high up the beach and later pulling far back. What is this called, and what mostly causes it?
realProblems.answer: 4 The Moon does not make its own light. It is grey rock that reflects the Sun's light toward us, like a mirror. Moonlight is really sunlight bounced off the Moon.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 4 The Moon does not make its own light. It is grey rock that reflects the Sun's light toward us, like a mirror. Moonlight is really sunlight bounced off the Moon.

realProblems.prompt: Yusuf looks at the full Moon and says, "The Moon is making its own light like a lamp." Correct him kindly and explain the real reason it shines.
realProblems.answer: 5 The Moon is waxing (growing). In about a week from a thin crescent it will reach the full Moon. From new to full takes about two weeks; a crescent grows through quarter to full.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 5 The Moon is waxing (growing). In about a week from a thin crescent it will reach the full Moon. From new to full takes about two weeks; a crescent grows through quarter to full.

realProblems.prompt: Amina keeps a Moon diary. On the first night she draws a thin crescent, and each night it grows fatter. What phase is coming in about a week?
realProblems.answer: Work through the task and check with your teacher or the reference card.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Work through the task and check with your teacher or the reference card.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Name the three spherical objects you have studied in this unit.
keyIdeas: Earth orbits the Sun once (a year)
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 1 Earth, the Sun, and the Moon. All three are the spherical objects studied in this unit.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: What is the axis of the Earth?
keyIdeas: Earth orbits the Sun once (a year)
Moon orbits Earth once (a lunar month)
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 2 An imaginary line through the middle of Earth, from top to bottom, that Earth spins around. Earth rotates around this line, causing day and night.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain in your own words what causes day and night.
keyIdeas: Earth orbits the Sun once (a year)
Moon orbits Earth once (a lunar month)
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 3 Earth spins on its axis. The half facing the Sun has day; the half turned away has night. As Earth turns, each place moves from light into shadow and back.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Where does the Moon's light really come from?
keyIdeas: Moon orbits Earth once (a lunar month)
Number of planets in the solar system
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 4 From the Sun. The Moon reflects the Sun's light toward Earth. The Moon makes no light of its own; it shines like a mirror.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: What is a lunar month, and about how many days does it last?
keyIdeas: Number of planets in the solar system
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 5 A lunar month is one full cycle of Moon phases, from new Moon to the next new Moon. It lasts about 29 to 30 days. This is the length of a month in the Islamic calendar.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Why does the new crescent Moon matter for Ramadan and Eid?
keyIdeas: Number of planets in the solar system
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 6 The Islamic calendar follows the Moon, so a new month begins when the new crescent is sighted. This marks the start of Ramadan and, later, Eid. Families watch the evening sky for the thin crescent.

questions.question: What shape are the Earth, the Sun, and the Moon?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: spherical All three are round balls, not flat. Photos from space and everyday observation show this.

questions.question: Which of these is the biggest?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: the Sun The Sun is by far the biggest; over a million Earths could fit inside it.

questions.question: Which of these is the smallest?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: the Moon The Moon is the smallest of the three, about one quarter as wide as Earth.

questions.question: What causes day and night on Earth?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Earth spinning Earth rotates on its axis, carrying us into and out of sunlight.

questions.question: How long does Earth take to spin around once?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: 24 hours One full spin takes 24 hours, which is one day and night.

questions.question: How long does Earth take to orbit the Sun once?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: one year Earth's orbit around the Sun takes about 365 days, which is one year.

questions.question: How long does the Moon take to orbit the Earth once?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: about one month The Moon orbits Earth in roughly 29 to 30 days.

questions.question: Why does the Moon shine at night?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: reflects the Sun's light The Moon is grey rock with no light of its own; it acts like a mirror.

questions.question: What do we call the different shapes the Moon seems to have?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Moon phases The changing shapes are called phases.

questions.question: What holds the Moon near the Earth and stops it drifting away?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: gravity Gravity is the invisible pull that keeps the Moon near Earth.

questions.question: When it is bright midday where you live, on the opposite side of Earth it is about:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: midnight The opposite side of the spinning Earth faces away from the Sun, so it is night.

selfAssessment: I can by the end of this unit, if you study carefully and try the activities, you will be able to do all of these things. Read them now, and read them again at the end to see how much you have grown.
I can describe Earth, the Sun, and the Moon as spherical (ball-shaped) objects, not flat.
I can compare the sizes of Earth, the Sun, and the Moon, and say which is biggest and which is smallest.
I can explain that Earth spins on its axis and that this spinning causes day and night.
I can explain that Earth orbits the Sun once each year.
I can describe how the Moon orbits Earth about once every month.
I can name and put in order the main phases of the Moon, from new Moon to full Moon and back.
I can explain why the Moon appears to change shape, using the idea of sunlight and shadow.
